Use ChatGPT Without Paying or Writing Code (2023 Snapshot)
ChatGPT has been everywhere lately and lots of people want to try it. This post walks through using ChatGPT — completely free, no coding required.

This post is from April 2023. The ChatGPT signup flow has changed since (no longer requires phone verification for most users), and the "data cutoff 2021" mentioned below is also outdated — newer models have far more recent training data. Keeping this post as a snapshot of early ChatGPT adoption. The prompting tips section still applies.
Quick context — ChatGPT is an AI model from OpenAI (an AI research lab). It lets you chat with an AI, and OpenAI has partnerships with major companies including Microsoft and GitHub.
Why is this AI such a big deal? Older AI tended to give cold, off-topic responses. ChatGPT replies feel much more like a human conversation:
ChatGPT is free (as of 2023/04/13 when I wrote this — that may change). You can pay if you want, but this post focuses on using it free.
to open ChatGPT. The page asks you to log in or sign up:
Here's the first-time signup flow — after this you'll just log in. Click "Sign up":
I'd recommend using a Google account since most people have one. Click "Continue with Google":
Enter your name and birthdate. Click Continue:
Enter a phone number for verification:
You'll get an SMS with a verification code. Paste it into ChatGPT and you're registered.
One phone number per ChatGPT account. If you've already registered, you can't register again with the same number.
After registering and logging in, you'll see:
Don't worry about the English UI — the layout is simple. Annotated version (originally in Chinese, but the structure is clear):
"New Chat" to start a fresh thread
How to chat? Just type into the input box and send. Example:
When you're done with one conversation and want to start another, click "New Chat".
Easy. You're now using ChatGPT — no subscription, no payment, completely free.
The one minor downside — no native localization, which can be intimidating if English isn't your strong suit. But the actual feature set you need is small.

Now for some tips on getting better responses. You might try ChatGPT and feel like "this isn't as useful as I expected" — the responses come back in English or Simplified Chinese, or not quite what you wanted.
ChatGPT is a generative language model — it generates responses based on what you give it. So you can give it rules upfront. Example — if you want ChatGPT to be your English tutor:

"From now on, you'll be my English tutor — your name is Judy and I'll be your student. Use Traditional Chinese and Taiwan-style phrasing to teach me English, and you don't need to repeat these rules."

Giving ChatGPT rules upfront gets you more consistent and useful responses. People sometimes call ChatGPT users "incantation wizards" because the right words and keywords are what unlock the right answers.
ChatGPT is free, but there are catches.
Don't use it for illegal stuff
You can't use ChatGPT to ask about:
…and similar. Try any of these and ChatGPT will refuse:
Free ChatGPT hits load limits during peak usage — you'll see errors, or slow responses. Solution? Pay for Pro.
(Though I personally didn't notice much speed improvement after paying.)
ChatGPT is heavily dependent on its training data. If the data doesn't cover what you're asking, the responses can be wrong. Don't trust 100% — use as reference.
ChatGPT needs massive training data, and that data has a cutoff date. At the time of writing, ChatGPT's training cutoff is 2021 — ask about anything since then and you'll get poor answers.
Given the training-data limit, you might wonder — why is ChatGPT still so popular?
The killer feature isn't just answering questions — it's quickly producing draft content. Example, draft a speaker outreach email:
Tweak the draft slightly and ship. Way faster than writing from scratch.
You can also use ChatGPT to learn new things, but you need enough background to evaluate whether what ChatGPT says is correct. OpenAI explicitly states:
ChatGPT sometimes writes plausible-sounding but incorrect or nonsensical answers.
So treat ChatGPT output skeptically. It's a powerful tool, but it can be wrong.
Final heads-up — ChatGPT is open to developers via API, which means scams. Common pattern — someone offers you a free chatbot service:
They say it's free, and once you join, the bot starts pushing ads or scams. The developer is using your engagement (and your data) to monetize.
So if you use any third-party "free ChatGPT" service, don't give it personal info or sensitive data. The bot might steal it, or scam you outright.

Heads up — this post is from April 2023, written when ChatGPT was newly available and people weren't sure how to register. The signup flow described here has changed since (it's much simpler now), and the noted "data cutoff is 2021" is also outdated as newer models have launched. The prompting tips section still applies. Worth keeping as a snapshot of early ChatGPT adoption.
